original_abstract: Building on the recent analytical solution to the multi-target Bayes recursion—the $\delta$-Generalized Labeled Multi-Bernoulli ($\delta$-GLMB) filter—this paper details efficient strategies for its practical implementation. In its standard form, each iteration of the $\delta$-GLMB filter requires update and prediction operations that generate weighted sums of multi-target exponentials with a prohibitively large number of terms. To address this computational bottleneck, we employ ranked assignment and $K$-shortest path algorithms to selectively truncate these sums, identifying the most significant components without exhaustive enumeration. Furthermore, we introduce computationally efficient look-ahead strategies, derived from the Probability Hypothesis Density (PHD) filtering framework, to further streamline processing. Finally, we provide a formal characterization of the $L_1$-error introduced into the multi-target density by these truncation methods, ensuring both performance and mathematical rigor.
humanized_abstract: The δ-GLMB filter for tracking multiple targets was recently derived as a solution to the multi-target Bayes recursion. This work focuses on developing efficient implementations of this new filter. Without specific approximations, the standard implementation of the δ-GLMB filter involves filtering and prediction steps, both of which involve computing weighted sums of multi-target exponentials with an unmanageably large number of terms. In this paper, we develop an efficient implementation that uses ranked assignment and K-shortest paths techniques to prune the sums and keep only a few of the largest terms. In addition, we developed look-ahead PHD filter implementations to further accelerate the computations. We derive a mathematical characterization of the L1-error that is introduced to the multi-target distribution as a result of pruning.

We derive a mathematical characterization of the L1-error that is introduced to the multi-target distribution as a result of pruning.
